coredata.dc:title: Sensitive determination of hardness and fluoride in ground water by a hybrid nanosensor based on aggregation induced FRET on and off mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xike
Wang, Jiahuan
Li, Yong
Yang, Chao
Lu, Liqiang
Nie, Yulun
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive detection and determination of hardness and fluoride in the ground water is an important issue for the water safety. The present work reports a sensitive, selective, rapid and visual hybrid fluorescent nanosensor by hybridizing carbon dots and hexametaphosphate capped gold nanoparticles through intrinsic interactions of the two components. The hybrid nanosensor can be used for hardness (Ca2+, Mg2+) detection with the fluorescence intensity decreasing at 439 nm and increasing at 608 nm via the aggregation induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer on mechanism. In addition, the hybrid nanosensor mixed with calcium ions can detect fluoride in water with a lower detection limit of 0.339 ppm with the fluorescence color recovery based on the precipitation induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer off process. Experimental results indicate that the as-synthesized hybrid nanosensor has shown efficient feasibility and practicality for hardness and fluoride detection in actual groundwater samples. Furthermore, a paper-based analytical device has been fabricated by immobilizing the hybrid nanosensor on the cellulose filter paper for the visual and on-site detection of fluoride in water. The hybrid nanosensor would provide new sensing materials and mechanisms for the determination and detection of hardness and fluoride in the groundwater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hardness detection
Fluoride detection
Hybrid nanosensor
Visual sensing
Paper-based analytical device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of hardness by the hybrid nanosensor
Sensitive and selective sensing of F− by the hybrid nanosensor
Paper-based analytical device and its visual F− detection ability
Sensitive determination of hardness and F− by the hybrid nanosensor
Selective detection by the hybrid nanosensor
Sensing mechanism for hardness and F− by the hybrid nanosensor
Determination of hardness and F− in ground water samples
Paper sensor for visual detection of F−

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41773126 ) and the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41521001 ) and International Science & Technology Cooperation Program of China ( 2014DFA20720 ) and the “Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities” .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CDs (a) and hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs (b) and hybrid nanosensor (c), FT IR spectra of CDs (d) and hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs (e) inset: HRTEM of CDs (a) and hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs (b).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs (a), hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs (b), the hybrid nanosensor (c), the mixture solution of hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+ (d), and the mixture solution of hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+ in the presence of F−(e). The inset images show the corresponding fluorescence colors under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Fluorescence spectra of the hybrid nanosensor solution with the addition of different concentrations of (a) Ca2+, (b) Mg2+, (c) mixture solution (Concentration ratio of Ca2+:Mg2+ is 5:2), (d) F−. All the inset images show the corresponding fluorescence color changes.

(a) Linear fitting curve of I 608/I 439 with respect to the hardness. (b) Linear fitting curve of I608/I439 with respect to the F− concentrations. (c) Linear fitting curve of I608/I439 with respect to the Ca2+ concentrations and (d) Linear fitting curve of I608/I439 with respect to the Mg2+ concentrations. All experiments were carried out in water.

The interference studies of the hybrid nanosensor toward F−. The black bars represent the fluorescence response of the mixed system of hybrid nanosensor sensor and Ca2+ to different anions. The white bars represent the fluorescence of the above solution upon subsequent addition of F−. All experiments were carried out in water.
Schematic illustration of the hybrid ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for visual detection of Ca2+ and F−.
The color change of the paper sensors with addition of different concentrations of F− (0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM, 400 μM). The images were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.
Spiked and recovery test of hardness and F− in ground water samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive determination of hardness and fluoride in ground water by a hybrid nanosensor based on aggregation induced FRET on and off mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel hybrid nanosensor from carbon dots and hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs was applied for hardness and fluoride detection with high sensitivity, selectivity and visual ability based on aggregation induced FRET on and off mechanisms.

Sensitive detection and determination of hardness and fluoride in the ground water is an important issue for the water safety. The present work reports a sensitive, selective, rapid and visual hybrid fluorescent nanosensor by hybridizing carbon dots and hexametaphosphate capped gold nanoparticles through intrinsic interactions of the two components. The hybrid nanosensor can be used for hardness (Ca2+, Mg2+) detection with the fluorescence intensity decreasing at 439 nm and increasing at 608 nm via the aggregation induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer on mechanism. In addition, the hybrid nanosensor mixed with calcium ions can detect fluoride in water with a lower detection limit of 0.339 ppm with the fluorescence color recovery based on the precipitation induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer off process. Experimental results indicate that the as-synthesized hybrid nanosensor has shown efficient feasibility and practicality for hardness and fluoride detection in actual groundwater samples. Furthermore, a paper-based analytical device has been fabricated by immobilizing the hybrid nanosensor on the cellulose filter paper for the visual and on-site detection of fluoride in water. The hybrid nanosensor would provide new sensing materials and mechanisms for the determination and detection of hardness and fluoride in the groundwater.

ce:section.ce:para: Ground water is the important and even the only source of drinking water in many areas, especially in the remote agricultural regions. However, the arbitrary discharge of various pollutants into the environment has resulted in serious pollutions to deteriorate the ground water quality, thus greatly endangered the environmental safety and human health [1,2]. Among the micropollutants, the detection of fluoride (F−) in ground water has padded increasing public attention due to the serious fluorosis, acute gastric and kidney problems induced by the high take of F− [3–6]. In addition, an interesting phenomenon can be noticed that high-fluoride in ground water usually accompanies with high hardness which may lead to trouble in home and industry for the formation of the deposits called lime scale. Additionally, those alkaline ions especially calcium and magnesium ions are the main cause of cardiovascular disease and would affect the health of humans and animals [7,8]. So the development of novel materials and methods to recognize and sense hardness and F− in ground water has received considerable attentions due to their important roles in the environmental, chemical and biological process [9–11].

Despite the increasing interest and endeavors, few methods have ever been reported on hardness and F− determination. The traditional EDTA titration method for hardness determination suffers from the sophisticated operation process, time consuming and vulnerable to operational errors [7]. Besides, the titration end point is hard to judge when interference ions exists, and the regents used such as potassium cyanide are highly toxic to generate harms for operators and environment. Some other instrumental approaches, such as UV–vis spectrophotometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry et al. have also been proposed for determining total hardness, but these methods are tedious and complicated [7,8]. In addition, the previous F− detection methods rely heavily on the fragile and expensive instrumentations, their endogenetic tedious sample pretreatments and low selectivity greatly hinder the field applications [12–17]. As a result of these deficiencies, the fluorescent sensors with high specificity and sensitivity, visual ability, ease and safety of handling have attracted considerable attentions, and a number of fluorescent sensors have ever been reported for F− detection [18–23]. These sensors are indeed innovative and sensitive, most of which are mainly based on the organic dyes with special ion recognition unites containing rich amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups that can bind strongly to F− [16,24–26]. However, these dyes based probes for F− are faced with the poor water solubility which severely restricts their application in water environment systems. And also the synthesis of the organic dyes is complex and expensive. More importantly, these organic probes are significantly influenced by the environmental factors and are not suitable for the direct visual observation with the naked eye [27]. Compared with traditional fluorescent probes for F−, quantum dots (QDs), as a kind of inorganic nanomaterials, have attracted more attentions owing to their unique optical properties including size tunable fluorescence, narrow and symmetric emission peak with a broad excited wavelength, and excellent photo stability [28,29]. For example, carbon dots (CDs) with a size of less than 10 nm have attracted extensive attentions due to their low toxicity, good water solubility and excellent photo stability, and have been used for ions detection [30,31]. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) have also been intensively reported due to their applications in the detection field [32–34]. And the construction of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) channel from CDs and Au NPs is the efficient solution for various analytes detection [35–37]. So it may be a suitable candidate to construct a novel nanosensor via CDs and gold nanoparticles for F− detection.

Herein, we have designed and prepared a novel fluorescent hybrid nanosensor by integrating the blue emissive CDs and orange emissive Au NPs capped with hexametaphosphate groups which can chelate with Ca2+ and Mg2+. The novel hybrid nanosensor has shown dual characteristic emissions at 439 nm and 608 nm, which can be ascribed to CDs and capped Au NPs. With the presence of Ca2+ or Mg2+, the fluorescence intensity decreased at 439 nm and increased at 608 nm accompanying with the fluorescence color transfer from blue violet to orange based on the aggregation induced FRET on mechanism. When F− was added into the hybrid nanosensor mixed with Ca2+, the fluorescence intensity increased at 439 nm and decreased at 608 nm with the fluorescence color recovery due to the FRET off effect. Furthermore, the paper-based analytical device based on the hybrid nanosensor has also been developed for the on-site and visual sensing of F− in water, and the new nanosensor can also be used for hardness and F− determination in the real ground water samples.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (HAuCl4), l-glutathione, ethylenediamine, citric acid, calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2) potassium chloride (KCl) sodium chloride (NaCl) aluminum chloride (AlCl3) copper chloride (CuCl2) ferric chloride (FeCl3) manganese chloride (MnCl2) zinc chloride (ZnCl2) cadmium chloride (CdCl2) cobalt chloride (CoCl2) sodium fluoride (NaF), sodium hexametaphosphate (Na6P6O18), sodium hydrogen sulfite (NaHSO3), potassium iodide (KI), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium bromide (NaBr), sodium nitrate (NaNO3) sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) sodium phosphate (Na3PO3) disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO3) sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO3) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd (ShangHai, China). All reagents and solvents of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm−1) was obtained from a Millipore water purification system and employed throughout the experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with excitation and emission slit width of 10 nm. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were conducted on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected by an EQUNOX 55 instrument using the diffuse reflectance scanning disc technique from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 at room temperature. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL 2000 EX transmission electron microscope operated at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained with a JEM-2100 FEF(UHR) high resolution transmission electron microscope. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were obtained on a Rigaku D/MAXRC X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source (45.0 kv, 50.0 mA). The zeta potential was recorded on a zeta potential and particle size analyzer (Malvern, nano ZS 90).

The fluorescent CDs were prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Typically, 5.0 g of citric acid was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the introduction of 0.5 mL of anhydrous ethylenediamine agitating vigorously for 15 min to form a clear solution. The mixture solution was then heated for 7 h at 180°С in the polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. The obtained brown solution was cooled down to room temperature and filtered twice through 0.22 μm membrane filter. After that, the filtrate was purified by dialyzing with ultrapure water using membrane (MW = 500) to obtain the CDs solution. The purified solution was freeze-dried into powder and measured, and the obtained CDs powder was dissolved in water again with 10 μg/mL and stored for further use.

All the glasswares were firstly washed with aqua regia and then rinsed with ultrapure water several times before use. The hexametaphosphate-capped Au NPs were prepared via a thermal reduction method. Typically, 0.0737 g of L-glutathione was dissolved in a mixture solution of 50 mL of ultrapure water and 150 μL of 1 M HAuCl4. After vigorous stirring for 30 min, the mixture solution was heated and refluxed for 30 min at 95°С, and the solution color transferred to yellow. The pH values of the obtained yellow solution was adjusted to 3–4, followed by the addition of 5 mL of ethanol. Then Au NPs can be obtained by centrifugation, and dissolved in phosphate buffer solution for further functionalizations. 10 mL of 20 mM sodium hexametaphosphate was added into the Au NPs buffer solution with vigorous stirring for 12 h at ambient temperature. The hexametaphosphate–capped Au NPs solution was transferred into the dialysis bag (MW = 500), and the dialysis of solution lasted over 48 h to obtain the functionalized Au NPs solution. The purified solution was freeze-dried into yellow powder, which was dissolved in water with 17.3 mg/mL for further use.

The hybrid nanosensor was prepared following the below procedures. Briefly, 20 μL of CDs (10 μg/mL) and 10 μL of hexametaphosphate-capped Au NPs (17.3 mg/mL) solution were thoroughly mixed in 1.97 mL ultrapure water. The mixture solution was kept under vigorous stirring for 10 min for the surface hybridization through reaction and interaction, and then the hybrid nanosensor was obtained and stored for further use. The fluorescence spectra of the hybrid nanosensor were collected, and it exhibited dual characteristic emissions at 439 nm and 608 nm under a single excitation of 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hardness by the hybrid nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: For sensitive detection of Ca2+ and Mg2+, various concentrations (0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45 mM) of Ca2+ and Mg2+ were respectively added into the hybrid nanosensor aqueous solution in a 2 mL spectrophotometer quartz cuvette. And then the fluorescence spectra were recorded from 380 nm to 680 nm after each addition under a single excitation wavelength of 350 nm and a slit width of 10 nm. The solution pH was kept neutral during the whole sensing experiment. With the increasing addition of Ca2+ and Mg2+, the fluorescence intensity decreased at 439 nm and increased at 608 nm accompanying with the fluorescence color changing from blue violet to orange due to the aggregation induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer process. To evaluate the total hardness of the water, the mixture ([Ca2+]:[Mg2+] = 5:2) was also added into the hybrid nanosensor aqueous solution in a 2 mL spectrophotometer quartz cuvette with the same condition as that of Ca2+ and Mg2+ alone. The total hardness is remarked by the equivalent of calcium carbonate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective sensing of F− by the hybrid nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensing sensitivity of the hybrid nanosensor for F−, different concentrations of F− (0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45, 0.50, 0.55, 0.60, 0.65 mM) were added into the mixture solution of hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+ (0.45 mM), and the fluorescence spectra were collected from 380 nm to 680 nm with the single excitation of 350 nm and slit width of 10 nm.

To further study the detection selectivity of the hybrid nanosensor for F−, the fluorescence properties of the hybrid nanosensor towards the environmental relative anions, such as Cl−, Br−, I−, HSO3 −, HCO3 −, Ac−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, HPO3 2−, PO3 3−, H2PO3 − et al. have been carefully examined at the same conditions of F−. In addition, the competitive selective ability of hybrid nanosensor has also been investigated. The interfering anions of 0.10 mM has been added into the mixture solution of hybrid nanosensor, Ca2+ (0.45 mM) and F− (0.05 mM), and the fluorescence measurements were conducted to investigate the detection selectivity via the fluorescence intensity ratio changes. All the fluorescence measurements were performed at least in triplicate under ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based analytical device and its visual F− detection ability
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of on-site and visual detection of F−, a simple and facile paper-based analytical device has been developed. The paper-based analytical device was fabricated according to the previous literature with minor modifications [38]. Typically, 1 g of poly vinyl alcohol (PVA-1788) was dissolved in 20 mL of water to obtain the clear solution. After that, 2 mL of the hybrid nanosensor solution and 100 μL of Ca2+ (0.01 M) was added into PVA solution, and stirred vigorously. Then the mixture solution was evenly spread out on the common cellulose filter paper and dried at room temperature for 1 h. The paper analytical device based on the hybrid nanosensor was successfully prepared. The visual detection of F− was carried out by the paper sensors immersing in the solution of different concentrations of F− (0, 100, 200, 300, 400 μM) at room temperature. After drying, the fluorescence color changes of the paper-based sensors can be shown under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The structure and fluorescence properties of the as-synthesized CDs and capped Au NPs were investigated by the fluorescence spectra, TEM, HRTEM, XRD and FT IR spectra. The water soluble CDs were prepared via a simple and facile hydrothermal method with a quantum yield of 30.12% which is much higher than that of many other reported CDs [39,40]. The capped Au NPs were synthesized by a thermal reduction method, and the quantum yield of Au NPs was calculated to be 24.51% which is close to most of the Au NPs reported in many references [41–43]. The morphologies and sizes of CDs and capped Au NPs were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, TEM and HRTEM image of CDs displays that the CDs are of with the narrow of about 5.0 nm, and a spherical morphology. XRD pattern of CDs shows that a characteristic peak appears in the range of 20–25° to be assigned to the (002) plane of graphite (Fig. S1), illustrating that the as-prepared CDs are of amorphous state [44–46]. In addition, FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1d) shows a peak at 3404 cm−1 assigned to the OH stretching vibration. The characteristic vibrations of CH stretching and deformation are located at 2945 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1. The absorption band at 1710 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching vibration. The FT IR analytical results demonstrate the existence of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. TEM and HRTEM image of capped Au NPs (Fig. 1b) shows that the Au NPs were readily dispersed in water and possess a good dispersity with the size of about 3.3 nm and a spherical morphology. The FTIR spectrum of Au NPs prepared with hexametaphosphate as stabilizers is shown in Fig. 1e. The characteristic absorption bands at 1281 cm−1, 1089 cm−1 and 982 cm−1 can be assigned to the PO2 − asymmetric stretching vibration, the outer phase symmetrical stretching vibration and P-O stretches of the main chain, respectively, which indicates that the hexametaphosphate groups have been successfully capped on the surface of Au NPs [47].

Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra are also applied for the characterization of the hybrid nanosensor. As displayed in Fig. 2 , the maximum emissions of CDs and capped Au NPs are at 439 nm and 608 nm, respectively. In the inset of Fig. 2, we can also obviously notice that the CDs and Au NPs in water solutions can emit intensive blue and orange fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, respectively. For the hybrid nanosensor which is obtained from CDs and capped Au NPs, its fluorescence spectra has shown the dual emissive characteristic peaks at 439 nm and 608 nm, which can be ascribed to the CDs and capped Au NPs. In addition, the hybrid nanosensor in water has emitted the strong blue violet fluorescence which further validates the successful preparation of the hybrid nanosensor. The fluorescence stability of the hybrid nanosensor has also been monitored by the changes of the fluorescence intensity ratios, I 608/I 439. It was found the fluorescence spectra and the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 608/I 439 of the hybrid nanosensor did not show any significant changes with time prolonging. Furthermore, the fluorescence color of the hybrid nanosensor in water also had no any changes, indicating that the hybrid nanosensor possesses the good physical and optical stability against photo bleaching (Fig. S2). The hybrid nanosensor can be used to detect F− and Ca2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. With the addition of Ca2+ into the hybrid nanosensor solution, the fluorescence intensity decreases at 439 nm and increases at 608 nm with the fluorescence color changing from blue violet to orange. And when F− is added into the mixture solution of the hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+, the fluorescence spectra would recover to its original state with the fluorescence color changing back to blue violet, implying that F− and Ca2+ detection can be accomplished by this kind of hybrid nanosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive determination of hardness and F− by the hybrid nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the influence of the single hybrid nanosensor towards the water hardness and F−, the fluorescent titration experiments of the two components, CDs and capped Au NPs of the hybrid nanosensor have been firstly performed. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of CDs has no any changes with the addition of Ca2+. And when F− is added, still no fluorescence changes can be found, showing that the CDs alone cannot be used for Ca2+ and F− detection. The hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs alone were also applied to assess Ca2+ and F− sensing ability. As shown in Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity at 608 nm of Au NPs increases with the gradual addition of Ca2+, and the fluorescence color changes from colorless to orange. The fluorescence intensity enhancement of the capped Au NPs induced from Ca2+ may be attributed to the salt- induced effect. The combination between Ca2+ and hexametaphosphate on the surface of Au NPs can reduce the negative charge of Au NPs, thus leading to the breaking of electrostatic repulsive and the aggregation of Au NPs, which can strengthen the fluorescence of the system. In addition, when F− is added, F− would preferentially react with Ca2+ to form the insoluble calcium fluoride, thus the fluorescence would be greatly quenched. As shown in Fig. S4b, the fluorescence intensity decreases in the presence of F− accompanying with the fluorescence color changing from orange to colorless. Though the capped Au NPs also can detection Ca2+ and F− in water, but the fluorescence color variations are quite obscure, which are not easy for visual observations. The development of the ratiometric hybrid nanosensor can solve the problems perfectly.

Fig. 3 represents the fluorescence titration of Ca2+ and Mg2+ by the hybrid nanosensor. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence emission at 608 nm from the capped Au NPs gradually increases upon the addition of Ca2+. Unlike the role of Au NPs in the hybrid nanosensor, the emission at 439 nm from CDs decreases with the increasing concentrations of Ca2+, resulting in the self-calibration and a successive fluorescence color change from blue violet to orange. Fig. 3b shows the results of Mg2+ addition into the hybrid nanosensor which display the similar phenomenon as that of Ca2+. Then the fluorescence response of the total hardness which is decided by the concentration of Ca2+ and Mg2+ towards the nanosensor was shown in Fig. 3c with the same fluorescence variation tendency as that of Ca2+ and Mg2+ alone. The results above indicate the ratiometric dual emissive hybrid nanosensor can be used for hardness determination and its fluorescence intensity ratio I 608/I 439 has a quite good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.982 (Fig. 4 a). And the detection limit of the hybrid nanosensor towards Ca2+ and Mg2+is calculated to be 16 μM (0.640 ppm) and 70 μM (1.680 ppm) on the basis of the equation: detection limit = 3SD/ρ, where SD is the standard deviation of blank measurement and ρ is the slope between the fluorescence intensity ratio versus Ca2+ and Mg2+ concentration (Fig. 4c, d).

It is generally known that a common strategy for the design of F− sensors is based on the displacement approach, in which sensors can bond to metal ions, and then release them again in the presence of F−. Therefore, we can further utilize the mixed system of the hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+ as a platform for F− detection. As shown in Fig. 3d, a remarkable reduction of fluorescence intensity at 608 nm and a prominent fluorescence enhancement at 439 nm upon the addition of F− into the mixed system of the hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+ can be found. And also an obvious continuous fluorescence color changing from orange to blue violet can be observed by the naked eye. But F− detection by the capped Au NPs alone is hardly to be distinguished by the naked eye, which clearly demonstrates the advantages of the ratiometric hybrid nanosensor over the sole Au NPs as a fluorescence probe. The fluorescence intensity ratio I 608/I 439 of the hybrid nanosensor decreased proportionately with increasing amounts of F− and a relationship can be set up between the fluorescence intensity ratio and F− concentrations. A linear relation ranging from 0 to 450 μM with a coefficient of 0.992 has been obtained (Fig. 4b), and the detection limit of the hybrid nanosensor towards F− is calculated to be 21 μM (0.339 ppm) on the basis of the equation: detection limit = 3SD/ρ, where SD is the standard deviation of blank measurement and ρ is the slope between the fluorescence intensity ratio versus F− concentration. Compared with the majority of reported fluorescence sensors, the detection limit of this hybrid nanosensor was much lower [31,48–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection by the hybrid nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the influence of environmental relevant ions in the hybrid nanosensor for hardness, the fluorescence properties of the hybrid nanosensor towards many more cations, such as Na+, K+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+ et al. have been carefully examined at the same conditions of Ca2+ and Mg2+. As shown in Fig. S5a and b, we noticed that the addition of those interfering ions into the hybrid nanosensor cannot increase the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 608/I 439 even though their concentrations are twice more than those of Ca2+ or Mg2+. The similar phenomena were also observed when the interfering ions were added into the mixed system of hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+ or Mg2+. Furthermore the fluorescence responses of the hybrid nanosensor-Ca2+ mixed system in the presence of interfering anions, such as Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, Ac−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, HPO3 2−, PO3 3−, H2PO3 − et al. whose concentration are even twice than that of F−, have been recorded. As shown in Fig. 5 , only the existence of F− can induce the decrease of fluorescence intensity ratio of I 608/I 439. Other interfering anions have no any influences on the fluorescence intensity ratio changes, indicating that the hybrid nanosensor has a good selectivity on F− detection. In addition, the anti-interference study of the hybrid nanosensor for F− detection has also been conducted. Fluorescence intensity ratios were studied by adding F− to the solution of hybrid nanosensor-Ca2+ mixed system and miscellaneous anions which are even two times higher molar to explore its utility as an anion-selective chromomeric sensor. The experimental results indicate that the coexistence of interfering anions have a negligible effect on hardness and F− sensing, suggesting that the high selectivity of the hybrid nanosensor for hardness and F− over other environmental interfering anions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for hardness and F− by the hybrid nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of the hybrid nanosensor toward hardness and F− has been studied (Fig. 6 ). For the hybrid nanosensor, dual characteristic emission peaks exhibit at 439 nm and 608 nm, which can be ascribed to CDs and capped Au NPs, and the solution shows intensive blue violet fluorescence color under 365 nm UV light. From the previous analysis, the hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs alone can be used to recognize Ca2+with the fluorescence enhancement due to that hexametaphosphate chelating groups on the surface of Au NPs can specifically combine with Ca2+to generate calcium polyphosphate complex. When Ca2+ was added into the hybrid nanosensor solution, the existence of Ca2+ would reduce their negative charge, leading to the breaking of electrostatic repulsive, and then induce the aggregation of hexametaphosphate-capped Au NPs (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. S7, TEM image of the hybrid nanosensor in the presence of Ca2+ validates that the Au NPs are aggregated with each other with the thick shell coating formed by Ca2+ and hexametaphosphate. So the fluorescence intensity at 608 nm would enhance in the presence of Ca2+. Furthermore, the emission spectrum of CDs shows a large overlap with the absorbance of capped Au NPs aggregates (Fig. S8), which means that there is a good match as an energy donor and acceptor. So the FRET process between CDs and Au NPs aggregate is possible, and the fluorescence intensity at 439 nm decreases. As a result, the fluorescence intensity changes at 439 nm and 608 nm in the presence of Ca2+ are basically due to the aggregation induced FRET mechanism which may be the same theory as that of Mg2+. When F− is added into the mixed system of the hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+, F− preferentially reacts with Ca2+ to form the insoluble calcium fluoride to exit the Au NPs aggregates [53,54]. Additionally, the F− addition would also increase the negative charge of the system (Fig. S7), and enhance the mutual repulsion of the Au NPs, thus the FRET process has been inhibited. Then the fluorescence intensities at 439 nm and 608 nm would recover to its original state due to the precipitation induced emission recovery process. Based on the above experimental results and analysis, the hybrid nanosensor from CDs and capped Au NPs can be applied for hardness, especially F− detection with high sensitivity and selectivity based on the aggregation induced FRET on and off mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hardness and F− in ground water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the reliability and practicality of the prepared hybrid nanosensor, the spike and recovery test was performed in groundwater samples from Jianghan Plain, China. The groundwater sample were first filtered through filter paper (D = 0.45 μm) to remove the insoluble solids. And then 2 mL of the hybrid nanosensor was added into the ground water samples, followed by the introduction of the mixture with different hardness (10, 20, 30 ppm). The hardness in the ground water can be determined by the hybrid nanosensor, and each example has been measured for three times. Then different concentrations of F− (50, 100, 150 μM) were added into the hybrid nanosensor-Ca2+ mixed system. The concentrations of F− in the ground water can be determined by the hybrid nanosensor, and each concentration has been measured for three times. The experimental results were displayed in Table 1 , the total hardness and the recoveries of hardness and F− in ground water were statistically close to 100% and fluctuated from 91.0% to 111.0%. The results show that the hybrid nanosensor is efficient for the hardness and F− determination in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper sensor for visual detection of F−
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of on-site and visual sensing of F−, a paper-based analytical device has been developed by dropping the mixture solution of the hybrid nanosensor and Ca2+ onto the filter paper. Then the filter paper was cut into strips and immersed into the solutions with different F− concentrations to observe the fluorescence changes. Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence color of the paper sensor with different concentration of F− (0, 100, 200, 300, 400 μM) under a 365 nm UV lamp. Clearly, the fluorescence color of the paper sensor changing from orange to blue can be observed, which are greatly dependent on the increasing amounts of F− added. Such a kind of paper sensor could be used for the portable, on-site and visual sensing of F−, and this fluorescence detection method has a great potential for the sensitive, selective and visual detection of F− in the practical applications.

In conclusion, we have designed and prepared a novel fluorescent nanosensor by hybridizing CDs and hexametaphosphate capped Au NPs. The hybrid fluorescent nanosensor was used for the detection of hardness (Ca2+, Mg2+) and fluoride based on the aggregation induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer on and off mechanism. The hybrid nanosensor can detect Ca2+, Mg2+ and F− in water with the detection limit of 16 μM (0.640 ppm), 70 μM (1.68 ppm) and 21 μM (0.339 ppm) accompanying with the obvious fluorescence color changes. In addition, we applied the hybrid nanosensor for hardness and F− detection in the ground water with good results, implying its efficient feasibility and practicality in the practical applications. Furthermore, we have also demonstrated that the hybrid nanosensor can be used for the facilitation of paper sensors, which shows potential applications for on-site, visual and rapid determination of F− for the actual environmental pollution control.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41773126) and the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41521001) and International Science & Technology Cooperation Program of China (2014DFA20720) and the “Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xike Tian is a Professor at the Faculty of Material Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). He got his Ph.D. degree from Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His current research interests include optical detection technology and prevention treatment research of hazardous substances in the environment.

Jiahuan Wang is currently pursuing her master degree in Faculty of Material Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). And her current research interests focused on the detection of the environmental pollutants.

Yong Li is a Lecturer at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). He has obtained his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University. And his current research interests mainly focus on the optical detection nanomaterials and processing techniques for environmental pollutants.

Chao Yang is an Associate professor at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). His research focused on optical detection technology on environmental pollutants.
Liqiang Lu is an Associate professor at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). His research focused on the environmental pollutant optical detection.
Yulun Nie is a Professor at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). His research interests focused on the removal of groundwater contaminants.
